metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section how `impact_plan`'s hard-override detection will be updated to recognise the new string grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) given that the existing `_SEV01` lookup returns `0.0` for any unknown key.
evidence: _SEV01: dict[str | None, float] = {"none": 0.0, "low": 0.33, "medium": 0.67, "high": 1.0}
has_override = any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)
Plan scope: 'impact_plan floor logic keyed on the ac_unverifiable grade value (missing/broken keep the floor; underspecified contributes <= 0.55 and never floors)'
findings.finding: `decide.impact_plan`'s hard-override detection (`has_override`) uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0`, where `_SEV01` is keyed on `{none, low, medium, high}`. The new grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) are NOT in `_SEV01`, so they all resolve to `0.0` — meaning the floor will NEVER fire for any of the new grades, silently defeating the core promise of the change.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py — `impact_plan` / `_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` / `_SEV01`
scenarios: A verifier emits `ac_unverifiable: missing_oracle` — `_SEV01.get('missing_oracle', 0.0)` returns 0.0, `has_override` is False, the 0.85 floor never fires, and the finding is silently under-scored.
Same for `broken_oracle` — the floor that is supposed to stay protective is bypassed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0` with a set-membership check against the new grade vocabulary, e.g. `attrs.get(a) in {"missing_oracle", "broken_oracle"}` for floor-triggering grades and `attrs.get(a) == "underspecified_oracle"` for the capped contribution path; or define a separate `_ORACLE_GRADE_WEIGHT` mapping alongside `_SEV01`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py` to Scope and update `enrich_operator_attested` so that only `underspecified_oracle` (not `missing_oracle` or `broken_oracle`) is treated as clearing-equivalent, or explicitly justify why all new grades should still be cleared.
evidence: if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none'): attrs['ac_unverifiable'] = 'none'
Plan scope lists only `decide.py`, `sidecar.py`, the schema/prompt, unit tests, and `docs/plan-review-gate.md` — `workflow_ops.py` is absent.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:enrich_operator_attested` hard-codes the guard `attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none')` and clears the axis to `'none'`. After this change all three new grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) are non-none and will be unconditionally cleared to `'none'` for any operator-attested AC — including `missing_oracle` and `broken_oracle`, which are supposed to keep the hard floor. This file is not in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py lines 113–116 (`enrich_operator_attested`)
scenarios: A finding against an operator-attested AC receives grade `broken_oracle` (a factually wrong verification command). `enrich_operator_attested` clears it to `'none'`, the floor never fires, and the real plan defect is silently dropped.
Similarly for `missing_oracle` — a genuinely untestable criterion is cleared even though it would be a real defect independent of the operator-attested tag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section how `decide.py` bridges the new string grade vocabulary `{none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}` to numeric impact contributions — e.g., introduce a separate `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_SEV01` lookup map and a per-grade override predicate — so that `broken_oracle`/`missing_oracle` scores ≥ 0.85 (floor fires) and `underspecified_oracle` scores ≤ 0.55 (floor does not fire), without relying on the existing `_SEV01` map that only recognises {none, low, medium, high}.
evidence: Plan scope: 'decide.py: impact_plan floor logic keyed on the ac_unverifiable grade value (missing/broken keep the floor; underspecified contributes <= 0.55 and never floors)'
decide.py line 48: `_SEV01: dict[str | None, float] = {"none": 0.0, "low": 0.33, "medium": 0.67, "high": 1.0}` — new grade strings are not in this map

decide.py lines 166-173: `contribs = [_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]` and `has_override = any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)` — both use _SEV01 uniformly across all axes including ac_unverifiable
ABSENCE: no per-axis lookup adaptation is described in the plan; the implementer must invent the bridging mechanism
findings.finding: The plan replaces ac_unverifiable's grade vocabulary from {none, low, medium, high} to {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}, but `impact_plan` in decide.py scores ALL seven plan-severity axes via the shared `_SEV01` map keyed on {none, low, medium, high}. The new grade strings are absent from `_SEV01`, so `_SEV01.get('underspecified_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, `_SEV01.get('broken_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, and `_SEV01.get('missing_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0. This silently breaks BOTH the MAX-contribution leg (`impact_sev`) and the `has_override` predicate for all three new grades — `broken_oracle` and `missing_oracle` (which must floor at 0.85) would not trigger the floor, and none of the three grades would contribute to `impact_sev`. The plan states the floor logic is 'keyed on the ac_unverifiable grade value' but never specifies how the new string grades are mapped to numeric scores or how the existing `_SEV01`/`_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` mechanism is adapted for the new vocabulary.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py; cross-ref decide.py lines 48, 166-173 (_SEV01 / _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES / has_override logic)
scenarios: A verifier emits ac_unverifiable='broken_oracle'; impact_plan calls _SEV01.get('broken_oracle', 0.0) → 0.0; has_override is False; the 0.85 floor does NOT fire — a genuine plan defect goes unblocked, silently defeating the stated intent.
A verifier emits ac_unverifiable='missing_oracle'; same failure mode — the 0.85 floor silently does not fire.

A verifier emits ac_unverifiable='underspecified_oracle'; _SEV01.get returns 0.0 which is correct for the floor (does not trigger it), but the axis also contributes 0.0 to impact_sev rather than a capped positive contribution ≤ 0.55 as the plan intends.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for decide.py: 'Introduce a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_GRADE` lookup (e.g. `{"none": 0.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.50, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "missing_oracle": 1.0}`) and update both the MAX-contribution leg and the `has_override` predicate to use it for `ac_unverifiable` specifically, leaving the `_SEV01` path unchanged for the other six axes. The `underspecified_oracle` numeric contribution (e.g. 0.50) must satisfy `min(0.50 * mult, ...) <= 0.55` under all amplifier settings so it can never reach the floor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the numeric mapping protocol for the three oracle-kind grades in `impact_plan` (e.g., a dedicated `_ORACLE_IMPACT` dict or a conditional branch) so an executor can implement the >= 0.85 floor for missing/broken and the <= 0.55 cap for underspecified without inventing the mechanism.
evidence: Plan: 'underspecified_oracle findings score <= 0.55 (below the 0.60 lowest blocking threshold)' and 'missing_oracle and broken_oracle findings floor at >= 0.85'
Code: `_SEV01: dict[str | None, float] = {"none": 0.0, "low": 0.33, "medium": 0.67, "high": 1.0}` in decide.py:48 — no oracle-kind keys
Code: `has_override = any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)` in decide.py:172 — new grade strings fall through to 0.0 (no override triggered)
Code: `contribs = [_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]` in decide.py:166 — same fallback
ABSENCE: plan specifies no new lookup table, conditional branch, or mapping protocol for the oracle-kind vocabulary in impact_plan
findings.finding: The new oracle-kind grades (underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle) are not in the existing `_SEV01` map in decide.py, yet `impact_plan` uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0)` to both compute the MAX contribution and detect the hard-override predicate. The plan specifies behavioral outcomes (missing/broken floor at 0.85; underspecified scores <=0.55) but never defines the numeric mapping or dispatch mechanism that achieves this — no new lookup table, no conditional branch, no sentinel value. An executor cannot derive the implementation without inventing the protocol.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py + What section (grade definitions)
scenarios: An executor modifies decide.py to accept the new enum but must invent how missing_oracle/broken_oracle map to a numeric value that triggers has_override — two reasonable choices (treat as 'high'=1.0, or add a separate sentinel) yield different code paths.

Without a specified mapping, a plausible implementation using a new _ORACLE_IMPACT map keyed on the three grade strings may or may not interoperate with the existing _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES MAX loop — the plan is silent on whether ac_unverifiable should still participate in the MAX aggregation at all.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry for decide.py: 'Replace the `_SEV01` lookup for `ac_unverifiable` with a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT` map: `{"missing_oracle": 1.0, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.55, "none": 0.0}`; the hard-override predicate checks `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT.get(attrs.get("ac_unverifiable"), 0.0) >= 0.85` (i.e., only missing/broken trigger it); `underspecified_oracle` contributes 0.55 to the MAX but never triggers the floor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name both `plan_review_verifier.md` AND `plan_review_verifier_agentic.md` in the Scope as requiring the updated three-grade vocabulary and graded-rule description.

Code: plan_review_verifier_agentic.md:73-74 — same stale vocabulary and auto-high rule
Plan scope: 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' (singular 'prompt') — does not explicitly name the agentic variant

findings.finding: Both verifier prompt files (plan_review_verifier.md and plan_review_verifier_agentic.md) currently define the `ac_unverifiable` axis with the old `none|low|medium|high` vocabulary and the rule 'HARD-OVERRIDE axis: any non-none marks the finding auto-high.' The plan scopes updating the verify rubric prompt but does NOT specify that BOTH prompt files must be updated — only 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' is listed. The agentic variant is a separate file with the same stale vocabulary and conflicting rule.
findings.location: Scope section: 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md:73-74
scenarios: An executor updates plan_review_verifier.md with the new three-grade vocabulary and examples but misses plan_review_verifier_agentic.md, leaving the agentic verifier operating on the old none|low|medium|high enum with the old auto-high rule — schema mismatch at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope whether `enrich_operator_attested` should be updated to protect `broken_oracle` and `missing_oracle` grades from being cleared (i.e., only `underspecified_oracle` is clearable by operator attestation, since observability is the only thing operator-attestation justifies).
evidence: Code: `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none'): attrs['ac_unverifiable'] = 'none'` — workflow_ops.py:115-116
Plan: 'broken_oracle: a stated proving command/symbol/count is factually wrong... KEEPS the 0.85 hard floor (the check is protective; these are real plan defects)'
ABSENCE: no mention of enrich_operator_attested in the plan's Scope or AC sections; no specification of which new oracle-kind grades are clearable vs. protected
findings.finding: The `enrich_operator_attested` function in workflow_ops.py clears `ac_unverifiable` to 'none' whenever its value is `not in (None, '', 'none')`. Under the new enum, a `broken_oracle` grade (meaning the stated verification command is factually wrong — a real plan defect, not an observability limitation) would be cleared by the operator-attested enrichment path, silently suppressing a genuine defect signal. The plan does not specify how `enrich_operator_attested` should behave with the new grade vocabulary.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:115-116 (enrich_operator_attested decision rule)
scenarios: A finding carries broken_oracle (wrong CLI path in a verification AC). The AC happens to be tagged [operator-attested]. enrich_operator_attested clears ac_unverifiable to 'none', suppressing the broken_oracle floor — contradicting the plan's stated intent that broken_oracle 'keeps the 0.85 hard floor'.

An executor updating enrich_operator_attested to handle the new vocabulary must independently decide whether broken_oracle should be clearable — an unspecified decision the plan body implies should NOT be clearable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to scope: 'Update `enrich_operator_attested` clearing predicate to protect hard-floor grades: only clear `ac_unverifiable` when its value is `underspecified_oracle` (not `broken_oracle` or `missing_oracle`, which are real plan defects independent of observability).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name both `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md` and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md` explicitly in the Scope as files that must be updated with the new three-grade definitions and worked examples.
evidence: plan_review_verifier.md:67: `- ac_unverifiable — ... HARD-OVERRIDE axis: any non-none marks the finding auto-high.`
plan_review_verifier_agentic.md:73: same definition with old none|low|medium|high vocabulary
Both files must change to define the three new oracle grades with worked examples (AC1).
findings.finding: The plan's Scope says 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' will be updated, but the two concrete prompt files that define `ac_unverifiable` with the old `none|low|medium|high` vocabulary (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md` and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md`) are not named. It is ambiguous whether both are intended edit targets.
findings.location: Scope — 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md line 67; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md line 73
scenarios: An implementer updates only one of the two verifier prompts; the agentic pass continues to emit old low/medium/high grades, bypassing the new schema validation.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the Scope explicitly names both `plan-review-verifier` and `plan-review-verifier-agentic` prompts as recipients of the three-grade vocabulary update, or explain why only one prompt requires it.
evidence: Plan says 'The verify rubric prompt defines all three grades with one worked example each' — singular 'prompt'.
src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 146 and 238 show TWO verify steps: `prompt: plan-review-verifier-agentic` and `prompt: plan-review-verifier`.
If the new grade vocabulary is only added to one prompt, the other branch will produce old-style grades (low/medium/high) that the new impact_plan logic cannot correctly differentiate.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the verify rubric prompt defines all three grades with one worked example each' (singular prompt), but the gate YAML (`src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`) uses TWO separate verifier prompts: `plan-review-verifier-agentic` (the agentic/code-grounded branch) and `plan-review-verifier` (the single-turn branch). If only one prompt receives the three-grade definitions and examples, the other verifier will continue emitting `none|low|medium|high` for `ac_unverifiable`, causing the new scoring logic in `decide.py` to receive unknown grade values and default them to the old behavior — silently defeating the split.
findings.location: Scope section: 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'
scenarios: A plan review with code-grounded findings routes to the agentic verifier; a non-code-grounded review routes to the single-turn verifier. If only one prompt is updated, half of all reviews use the wrong vocabulary.

An ac_unverifiable grade emitted as 'low' (old vocabulary) by the un-updated prompt would be unrecognized by the new _SEV01-equivalent lookup for the three-way split and would fall into undefined behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' in the Scope to 'the plan-review verification output schema + BOTH verify rubric prompts (plan-review-verifier and plan-review-verifier-agentic)' and add each to the AC1 verification surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or justify why one is not needed) for the plan-v3 schema and scoring change — e.g. a feature flag, a v2-compatible reader that tolerates the new enum values, or a documented procedure for reverting sidecars written under plan-v3.
evidence: 'IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bumps (plan-v2 -> plan-v3) per ADR 0036 so calibration corpora never pool across the change' — version isolation is addressed, but rollback is not
ABSENCE: no back-out plan, no expand-contract sequence, and no description of how to revert sidecar findings or scoring behavior if plan-v3 is defective
AC3 pins the version bump by test but does not mention a rollback gate or feature flag
findings.finding: The plan changes the impact-scoring formula and the sidecar schema (new closed enum, new floor behavior) — a breaking change to the verification model — but states no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence. Referencing ADR 0036 for corpus isolation is not a rollback mechanism: if plan-v3 is deployed and produces regressions, there is no described path to revert without also addressing any sidecars already written under plan-v3, any in-flight reviews, or consumers that now expect the new enum values.
findings.location: Scope (sidecar.py, decide.py) and Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: plan-v3 is deployed; an unforeseen regression in blocking rates is detected; the team needs to revert but sidecars already written under plan-v3 carry new enum values that plan-v2 readers do not understand
in-flight plan reviews at the v2/v3 boundary produce mixed-version sidecars with no defined handling
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small 'Rollback' subsection: (1) the new enum values are additive to the sidecar key (existing readers skip unknown values gracefully — confirm or fix this); (2) a feature flag or config toggle gates the new floor logic so plan-v2 behavior can be restored without a code revert; (3) any sidecars written under plan-v3 during the rollback window are marked invalid and excluded from calibration via the version field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the lifecycle for plan-v2 REVIEW_RESULT sidecars: specify whether existing files are migrated, archived, or whether all sidecar consumers guard on IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION before interpreting the ac_unverifiable grade field — and add this to the AC or Scope.
evidence: 'calibration corpora never pool across the change' — implies version is used to segregate corpora, but only addresses the calibration consumer
ABSENCE: no mention of a migration script, a read-time version guard in consumers, or a documented tolerance policy for plan-v2 sidecars
The old ac_unverifiable axis had different values (binary-ish per 'Why'); those values will not be in the new closed set
findings.finding: The plan bumps IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION to plan-v3 and changes the persisted grade vocabulary for ac_unverifiable, but does not specify how consumers of already-stored plan-v2 REVIEW_RESULT sidecars should behave when they encounter the old grade values — nor whether old sidecars need migration or whether consumers must tolerate mixed-version files.
findings.location: Scope — src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py; 'IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump to plan-v3'
scenarios: A dashboard or reporting tool reads both plan-v2 and plan-v3 sidecars and encounters old ac_unverifiable values not in the new enum — it either errors or silently miscounts.
A calibration re-run accidentally ingests plan-v2 sidecars without version filtering and corrupts the calibration-4 baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to reflect all three grades (e.g., 'Split ac_unverifiable hard-floor axis: missing_oracle / broken_oracle / underspecified_oracle') so the title faithfully represents the three-way split documented in the body.
evidence: Title: 'Split ac_unverifiable hard-floor axis: missing_oracle vs underspecified_oracle'
Body: 'Replace the binary-ish ac_unverifiable severity grading with a THREE-value oracle-kind grade'
Body: 'broken_oracle: a stated proving command/symbol/count is factually wrong... (37% of sample) - KEEPS the 0.85 hard floor'
The title names only two of the three grades, omitting broken_oracle entirely
findings.finding: The plan title says 'missing_oracle vs underspecified_oracle' (implying a binary split), but the body introduces three grades — missing_oracle, broken_oracle, and underspecified_oracle — where broken_oracle is a distinct, non-trivial category (37% of sample) with its own floor behavior. A reader skimming the title will not anticipate the broken_oracle grade or understand why it keeps the hard floor.
findings.location: Title / ## What section
scenarios: A downstream reader (e.g., story ea39 author, calibration-4 planner) references the title to understand scope and designs around a 2-way split, missing the broken_oracle category and its floor-keeping rationale.
Documentation writers updating docs/plan-review-gate.md use the title as a summary and underspecify the broken_oracle case.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Split ac_unverifiable hard-floor axis into three oracle-kind grades (missing / broken / underspecified)' or similar that names all three values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit unit test that submits an invalid ac_unverifiable grade value (e.g. a string outside the closed set) to the schema and asserts it is rejected, covering the failure path stated in AC1.
evidence: Plan scope: 'schema round-trip' — classic happy-path only
AC1 asserts 'anything else rejected' as a behavioral requirement but no corresponding test is named

The closed vocabulary {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} → rejection of any other value is a distinct failure-path that is user-facing (the pass-2 verifier submits grades; a bad grade must be caught)
findings.finding: The unit tests listed cover 'schema round-trip' (valid grade values serialize/deserialize correctly) but do not explicitly include a test that an invalid grade value is REJECTED — the failure path of the schema validation contract.
findings.location: Scope > Unit tests; AC line 1 ('schema-validated; anything else rejected')
scenarios: Pass-2 verifier emits a typo grade (e.g. 'underspecified') — without a rejection test the schema may silently coerce or ignore it, causing wrong impact scoring that goes undetected
A future refactor renames a grade constant — the round-trip test passes but rejection of the old name is never verified
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parametrized pytest case: for each of several invalid strings (e.g. 'underspecified', 'none_value', 'MISSING_ORACLE', ''), assert that the schema validator raises a validation error, and confirm the error message identifies the field and the closed vocabulary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py` to Scope and enumerate which existing tests need to be updated or replaced to use the new three-grade vocabulary (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`).
evidence: assert impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'low'}) == 0.85  # line 40
assert impact_plan({axis: 'low'}) >= 0.85, axis  # line 50 (ac_unverifiable in loop)
Plan scope: 'Unit tests: impact pins for all three grades + none; schema round-trip; ...' — names new tests but not this existing file.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py` contains multiple tests that inject the old ordinal grade values (`'low'`, `'high'`, `'medium'`) for `ac_unverifiable` (e.g. `impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'low'}) == 0.85`, `impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'high'})`). After the schema change these values are no longer valid for that axis. These tests are not listed in Scope and will break or silently pass on the wrong vocabulary.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py lines 39–50, 91, 107, 129, 135, 145, 150
scenarios: If the new `impact_plan` rejects unknown ac_unverifiable values, existing tests will error. If it silently maps them, the regression pins no longer test the right vocabulary, giving false confidence.

Plan scope section names `sidecar.py` (IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump) but does not name `test_impact_model_versioning.py`.

scenarios: CI fails on `test_plan_review_payload_stamps_version` immediately after the sidecar constant is changed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope note clarifying the compatibility boundary: specify whether the schema validation applies only at the verifier output ingestion point (so old sidecar records with {low, medium, high} are read tolerantly) or at all read points; update or quarantine the existing test fixtures that set ac_unverifiable='high'/'low' so CI does not break on the new closed vocabulary.
evidence: AC1: 'The verification model accepts exactly the closed grade set {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for ac_unverifiable (schema-validated; anything else rejected)'
test_impact_plan.py line 40: `impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'low'}) == 0.85` — 'low' is not in the new closed set
test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py line 48: `verifs = {0: {'severity_attributes': {'ac_unverifiable': 'high'}, ...}}` — 'high' is not in the new closed set
sidecar.py lines 162-165: `latest_review_result` accepts plan_review_result_v1 and _v2 schemas — old sidecars with ac_unverifiable='high' are still read back under the new code
ABSENCE: no migration path or backward-compat handling for old vocabulary in existing test fixtures and sidecar records is described
findings.finding: The plan does not address how the new schema validation (AC1: 'anything else rejected') interacts with existing sidecar records and test fixtures that carry the old ac_unverifiable vocabulary ({low, medium, high}). The `enrich_operator_attested` function tests `attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none')` and existing unit tests (e.g. test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py, test_impact_plan.py) set `ac_unverifiable: 'high'` or `'low'`. Once the schema rejects old vocabulary values at ingestion, these test fixtures become invalid under the new contract — but the plan says the grade persists 'losslessly in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar per finding' and the sidecar reader accepts plan_review_result_v2 records that pre-date this change. There is no migration or compatibility path stated.
findings.location: Scope: 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'; AC1; cross-ref workflow_ops.py line 115, test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py lines 48/59/69/82, test_impact_plan.py lines 39/91/107
scenarios: Existing unit tests that set ac_unverifiable='high' or 'low' fail schema validation if the new schema is applied at the test level, breaking the CI suite.

The sidecar reader returns an old plan-v2 record with ac_unverifiable='high' to the novelty or recall subsystem; downstream code that reads severity_attributes passes 'high' to impact_plan, which now has no mapping for it (0.0 from _SEV01), silently mis-scoring the priority of a recalled finding.

The enrich_operator_attested guard `not in (None, '', 'none')` still triggers for old vocabulary values ('high', 'low') and clears them to 'none' — this is accidentally correct behavior but is undocumented and fragile.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to ACs 1–3 (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/tests/test_impact_floor.py -v` or equivalent) so each AC's pass/fail is deterministically verifiable without guessing test locations.
evidence: 'Impact scoring pinned by unit tests: missing_oracle and broken_oracle findings floor at >= 0.85; underspecified_oracle findings score <= 0.55'
'grade value is persisted per finding in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, pinned by a test'
Scope names test categories ('impact pins for all three grades + none; schema round-trip; sidecar persistence') but no file path or pytest invocation appears anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: ACs 1–3 each describe a unit-test–based verification outcome ('pinned by unit tests', 'schema-validated', 'grade value is persisted … pinned by a test') but provide no concrete proving command — no specific pytest invocation, test-file path, or CLI flag. An executor can infer `pytest`, but without a path or test-name anchor the proving command is not concrete enough to produce unambiguous pass/fail evidence per E6(a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — ACs 1, 2, 3
scenarios: An executor writes tests in the wrong module or under a different name; CI passes but the ACs remain unverified because no anchor ties the AC to a specific test identity.
A reviewer cannot independently confirm completion without guessing the test file path.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Proving command: `pytest src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/tests/test_impact_floor.py -v` — expect four parameterised cases (none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle) all green.' Mirror the pattern for AC1 (schema round-trip test path) and AC3 (sidecar persistence test path).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC4 for the doc-content sub-claim, e.g., `grep -i 'calibration-3\|calibration_3' docs/plan-review-gate.md` returning a match, so the citation requirement is independently verifiable separate from the Gerrit vote.
evidence: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md documents the three-grade rule citing the calibration-3 evidence comment on this story, and the change is MERGED through Gerrit (LLM-Review +1, Verified +1)'
No grep or diff command is given for verifying the citation text; the Gerrit vote is process-verifiable but the doc content is not anchored to a command.
findings.finding: AC4 bundles two distinct verification requirements — (a) correct doc content in docs/plan-review-gate.md and (b) a Gerrit merge with specific votes (LLM-Review +1, Verified +1) — without a proving command for the doc-content claim. The executor has no specified grep/diff command to confirm the calibration-3 citation is present, so the doc-content sub-claim lacks a concrete in-session check per E6(a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
scenarios: The documentation is merged with the version bump prose but the calibration-3 citation is accidentally omitted; the Gerrit +1 still lands and the AC is claimed complete.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Proving command (doc content): `grep -iE "calibration.?3" docs/plan-review-gate.md` — must match at least one line; Gerrit merge vote is verified via the change-id recorded on this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the underspecified_oracle impact cap and the hard-floor value as named, centrally-located constants (e.g. `UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_MAX_IMPACT`, `AC_UNVERIFIABLE_FLOOR`) in decide.py (or a shared thresholds module), so a single-site update suffices when calibration cycles revise blocking thresholds.
evidence: AC line 2 asserts fixed numeric bounds (0.85, 0.55, 0.60) without specifying whether they are named constants or inline literals in decide.py
'the other three override axes ... calibration 4 scope' — plan explicitly signals more calibration cycles are coming

'the lowest blocking threshold in force after calibration 3 (0.60)' — the blocking threshold itself has a calibration history, implying 0.55 (derived as 'below 0.60') will need updating if the blocking threshold moves
findings.finding: The underspecified_oracle impact cap (<=0.55) and the hard floor (>=0.85) are calibration-derived numeric thresholds that will be embedded in decide.py floor logic. The plan explicitly acknowledges ongoing calibration cycles (calibration 4 is named as future scope for other axes), yet does not specify whether these values are configurable constants/named symbols or will be hardcoded literals — making them fragile when calibration cycles revise blocking thresholds.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (Scope); AC line 2 ('missing_oracle and broken_oracle findings floor at >= 0.85; underspecified_oracle findings score <= 0.55')
scenarios: Calibration 4 lowers the blocking threshold to 0.50 — the underspecified_oracle cap of 0.55 now exceeds it and auto-blocks again, requiring a manual grep across decide.py to find all hardcoded 0.55 references
A future story bumps plan-v3→v4 and must chase down numeric literals rather than updating a single named constant
findings.suggested_fix: In decide.py introduce module-level constants: `AC_UNVERIFIABLE_FLOOR = 0.85` and `UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_MAX_IMPACT = 0.55` (with a comment tying the latter to 'must remain below BLOCKING_THRESHOLD_MIN = 0.60'). Reference these in both the floor logic and the unit-test assertions so calibration updates are a one-line change with a clear dependency comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling path when schema validation rejects an unrecognized ac_unverifiable grade: define whether to default to `missing_oracle` (safe/conservative), surface a structured error, or abort the finding with a logged warning — and confirm the failure is observable (log/metric/alert).
evidence: 'schema validation accepts exactly {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for the axis'
'anything else rejected' in AC — but no fallback or error-surfacing behavior is described
ABSENCE: no mention of retry, fallback grade, or operator-visible error when an out-of-vocabulary value arrives
findings.finding: The plan adds schema validation that rejects any ac_unverifiable grade value outside {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}, but specifies no error-handling path for when the LLM verifier returns an unrecognized grade string — it is unspecified whether the finding is dropped, defaulted to a safe value, or causes the entire review run to fail.
findings.location: Scope — verification output schema + verify rubric prompt; AC line 1 ('schema-validated; anything else rejected')
scenarios: A new LLM model or a prompt regression causes the verifier to emit a synonym or misspelling; schema validation fires; behavior is undefined — review may silently drop the finding or crash the pipeline.
A partial LLM response truncates the grade field; validation rejects it; callers receive no signal about which finding was lost.
findings.suggested_fix: In decide.py / schema validation layer, catch invalid-grade exceptions and either (a) default to `missing_oracle` with a WARN log + metric increment so the conservative floor still applies and operators are alerted, or (b) reject the whole finding with an ERROR log. Document the chosen behavior in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a migration/compatibility strategy for existing plan-v2 sidecar records (e.g., a compat read shim that maps legacy values, or an explicit 'ignore pre-v3 ac_unverifiable values' policy), and assert the exact schema state of the sidecar store after a rollback from plan-v3 to plan-v2.
evidence: 'schema-validated; anything else rejected' — AC1 implies hard rejection of values outside the new vocabulary, which breaks reads of existing plan-v2 sidecar records
'values change, key stays' — Scope section confirms this is an in-place value-level schema change on existing persisted keys
ABSENCE: no mention of a compat read shim, migration script, or expand-contract approach for existing sidecar records
ABSENCE: no assertion on schema state after rollback from plan-v3 to plan-v2 (POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE requirement)
findings.finding: The plan changes the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar schema (ac_unverifiable values change from the old binary-ish set to {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}) and enforces strict schema validation ('anything else rejected'), but provides no migration path, backward-compatibility read layer, or rollback state description for existing plan-v2 sidecar records. Old records with legacy grade values will fail the new schema validation when read by plan-v3 code. Additionally, if plan-v3 is rolled back after new sidecars have been written, the plan does not assert what schema state exists post-rollback — plan-v2 code will encounter unrecognized plan-v3 grade values with no defined handling.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py; AC1 ('schema-validated; anything else rejected'); AC3 ('grade value is persisted per finding in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar')
scenarios: Downstream tooling (calibration, reporting) opens an existing plan-v2 sidecar under plan-v3 code and fails schema validation, breaking historical corpus reads

plan-v3 is rolled back to plan-v2 after some sidecars are written with new grade values; plan-v2 code encounters unknown values and has no defined error handling, potentially corrupting calibration runs
findings.suggested_fix: Add an expand-contract note: (1) plan-v3 reader accepts both old and new grade values during a transition window (legacy values treated as 'underspecified_oracle' or silently ignored); (2) a one-time idempotent migration script re-grades existing plan-v2 records if needed, or explicitly documents that pre-v3 records are excluded from schema validation (corpus isolation already achieved by IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION). For rollback: assert that post-rollback plan-v2 code falls back to treating unrecognized grade values as 'none' (safest no-floor state) rather than failing hard.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
